title: Welcome!
text:
I am happy to have you here!
This is where I document my path toward computational biology and bioinformatics: pipelines I’m building, things I’m learning, and the process behind the research (not the findings themselves).



Lab Notebook — dated entries on what I’m working through, week to week
About — background, skills, and email contact if you need to contact me for any questions etc!

title: Docking using T4 lysozyme L99A
text:
I ran my first validated docking experiment:
“Docking benzene into the hydrophobic cavity of T4 lysozyme L99A (PDB 181L) using AutoDock Vina”
I picked this system because the crystal structure already has benzene bound, so I could check my pipeline against a known answer to check if my docking was a success.
The top-scoring pose lined up closely with the crystallographic benzene confirmation the pipeline (receptor prep → protonation → docking → pose comparison) actually works end to end, not just that the software ran without errors. This matters because every later result is only as trustworthy as this baseline check.
During my follow-up screening panel (benzene, toluene, hexylbenzene, phenol, ethanol against the same hydrophobic pocket), phenol’s docking score didn’t make sense. Its affinity was more than expected. Phenol has a hydroxyl group, so my first assumption was that its score must be coming from a hydrogen bond with a polar residue in the pocket. I thought that would be the obvious story for a molecule with an -OH group.
Vina’s score didn’t show a desolvation penalty for phenol’s buried hydroxyl group either, which is a known blind spot of simple empirical scoring functions. Automatically, I thought this is not necessarily a real thermodynamic fact about the system itself.
Instead of assuming, I wanted to actually check it so I measured the actual distances between phenol’s hydroxyl and nearby residues in PyMOL. Hydrogen bonds have a fairly strict cutoff roughly 3.5 Å between H-bond donors and acceptors. Nothing in the pocket was within that range. So the H-bond explanation didn’t make sense once I actually measured it.
What was really driving the score turned out to be the same thing as benzene and toluene, which was hydrophobic packing against the cavity walls. The -OH group was just there, it was not doing the work I’d assumed.

title: About
text: Pharmacology student, working toward computational biology / bioinformatics with a longer-term interest in computational neuroscience. I learn best by building things, I wanted to document my journey.

title: About
text:
What I’m working on

Molecular docking pipeline (AutoDock Vina, HADDOCK) for a neuroscience-adjacent research project. See the Lab Notebook for process notes (results/findings are kept off this site)
RNA-seq analysis, variant calling, and NGS workflows
Building reproducible, documented pipelines

title: About
text:
Skills
Computational: Python, Galaxy, conda environments
Bioinformatics tools: AutoDock Vina, PDB2PQR, OpenBabel, Meeko, PyMOL, HADDOCK
In progress: RNA-seq pipelines, variant calling, scientific programming

title: About
text:
Outside the lab
Digital design and pixel art — most of the visual design on this site (illustrations, icons, layout touches) is my own work.
Oil painting — I enjoy relaxing while inhaling fumes, it does good for my mind
By the way, I will also be making many different stained glass pieces in gothic / victorian style. Including, but not limited to:

mirror frames
lampshades
figures

I will update it here soon and I will link my etsy and showcase account here soon, so if you are interested, stay tuned !
